Fixes #1881 
Depends on #2069 

- Fixes upload_date for videos not being in UTC timezone (by parsing the `dateText` instead)
    - player response upload date is fixed to a timezone such as PT (US timezone). We will only use this as a fallback now.
    - this inadvertently fixes upload dates not being available on non-web clients (#1067)
    - For videos where there may be relative time, this would be restricted to <24 hours, so will still be accurate.
    - Livestreams, premieres and scheduled videos will continue to use microformat upload date, since in those cases the upload date is the true upload date. This changes to published date once the video is published.
~~- Fixes `datetime_from_str` using computer's timezone rather than UTC~~ already merged
   ~~- relative time text parsing actually returns a UNIX timestamp in UTC~~
~~- Enforce UTC timezone for all API/webpage calls in YouTube extractors, regardless of cookie setting.~~ already merged

- [ ] I am not the original author of this code but it is in public domain or released under [Unlicense](http://unlicense.org/) (provide reliable evidence)

### What is the purpose of your *pull request*?
- [ ] Bug fix
- [x] Improvement
- [ ] New extractor
- [ ] New feature

---

### Description of your *pull request* and other information

I'm still getting some file access errors on my laptop, so I extended the logic to cover a few more places. I added `try_remove`, matching `try_rename` to avoid bare `os.remove` calls. To avoid code duplication I turned the retry logic into a decorator that wraps `sanitize_open`, `try_remove` and `try_rename`.

Notes:
- `try_rename` had a custom error message. I couldn't add it to the decorator and I think it would have conflicted with the error handling so it now raises the built in error if it fails.
- I added `error.EINVAL` (error 22) to the list of errors to retry, as I also saw this error on my laptop (I'm pretty sure it's the same problem as the parameter looks fine and it doesn't fail every time).

* Simplify regex to avoid catastrophic backtracking, noticed after #2129
  * Loosely match anything between the tag and the AV-pair of interest (authored by @pukkandan)
  * Only match up to the AV-pair of interest, rather than the end of the end of the opening tag
  * Strictly match an unquoted value
  * Likewise determine if quotes are optional around the matched value
  * Avoid `re.DOTALL` as `.` is not used
* Yield directly in `re.finditer`, rather than append to and return a list
  * Noop as both calling functions perform list comprehension
  * But adapt direct calls in `test_utils.test_get_elements_text_and_html_by_attribute`

This is an extractor for "WatchESPN" videos, i.e. anything on [this page](https://www.espn.com/watch/schedule/_/type/replay/startDate/20220108) not listed as ESPN+. All applicable videos require TV provider and are probably geolocked to United States. As a consequence, the base ESPN extractor has been updated to not match WatchESPN URLs and its WatchESPN tests have been removed.

This pull request should be fully functional as-is but it could use the following improvements:

- Addition of a test to check that ESPN+ videos raise the appropriate error. I tested this on my own but I am not sure how to codify this as a test within yt-dlp.
- Additional metadata to return (e.g. description, dates, etc.). Accessing such information requires calls to a different API pointer which I didn't bother to code in.
- It would be useful if one of the more veteran yt-dlp devs could check that I am following appropriate style/abstraction guidelines.

There is no specific issue raised for this (except for possibly [this one on youtube-dl](https://github.com/ytdl-org/youtube-dl/issues/25305)) but I feel adding this extractor would be immensely useful considering that:

- ESPN is a very popular platform with replays available of many major sporting events and other TV shows, for those with access to ESPN through their cable TV provider.
- Before about August, such videos could only be downloaded by opening up the video and snooping for the playlist m3u8.
- Since August, ESPN has updated their platform so that only dash DRM videos play. Downloading an unprotected video required making modifications to the snooped URL, which had to be done quickly as the authorization expires quickly. Now, with this extractor, unprotected HLS streams are returned by default, saving many steps for anyone wishing to download these videos.

body:
Rather than allowing a missing channel tab to silently redirect to the Home tab, raise an error instead. This should result in less surprising behavior.

### What is the purpose of your *pull request*?
- [x] Bug fix
- [x] Improvement
- [ ] New extractor
- [ ] New feature

---

### Description of your *pull request* and other information

See one use case in https://github.com/yt-dlp/yt-dlp/issues/2306 . There is existing code that special cases /featured and /live tabs which either redirects or raises an error. The code suggests that it was meant to warn on all missing tabs, however only /videos would be checked.

This PR generalizes the tab name check and raises an error if the tab does not exist (or is hidden to the user due to a lack of access). Any tab not specially handled and which does not exist will trigger an error. All changes are grouped under the existing compat option "no-youtube-channel-redirect", so those who set this option will see no difference in behavior.

The changes do not include a check for whether certain video views such as "Upcoming live streams" exist; such changes would be less trivial to implement. The current behavior for these URLs haven't changed, you will still be "surprised" if no such streams exist.

### Description of your *pull request* and other information

This patch adds support for ERTFLIX videos and series (optionally filtered by season) and embedded video extraction from ert.gr webtv. ERT (Hellenic Broadcasting Corporation) is the public broadcaster in Greece.

* Add new IEs:
  * ERTFlixBase: Base IE class
  * ERTFlixCodename: Generic video by codename extractor, it matches virtual `ertflix:<codename> URLs
  * ERTFlix: Extract episodes and series (optionally filtered by season or episode group), it resolves "tiles" and defers to ERTFlixCodename
  * ERTWebtvEmbed: Extract iframe-embeddable ert.gr webtv videos
    * Resolve stream and thumbnail URLs from templates hard-coded in the player page
    * Player page provides almost no metadata, so let the generic extractor override title

Pull https://github.com/ytdl-org/youtube-dl/pull/30528 with changes.

body:
### Before submitting a *pull request* make sure you have:
- [X] At least skimmed through [contributing guidelines](https://github.com/yt-dlp/yt-dlp/blob/master/CONTRIBUTING.md#developer-instructions) including [yt-dlp coding conventions](https://github.com/yt-dlp/yt-dlp/blob/master/CONTRIBUTING.md#yt-dlp-coding-conventions)
- [X] [Searched](https://github.com/yt-dlp/yt-dlp/search?q=is%3Apr&type=Issues) the bugtracker for similar pull requests
  - This is a port of [Youtube-DL#29160](https://github.com/ytdl-org/youtube-dl/pull/29160) to yt-dlp
  - [Youtube-DL#26778](https://github.com/ytdl-org/youtube-dl/pull/26778) is similar, but with less features. I have talked to @ruru4143, and he has suggested creating a new PR for this and closing the old one
- [X] Checked the code with [flake8](https://pypi.python.org/pypi/flake8)

### In order to be accepted and merged into yt-dlp each piece of code must be in public domain or released under [Unlicense](http://unlicense.org/). Check one of the following options:
- [X] I am the original author of this code and I am willing to release it under [Unlicense](http://unlicense.org/)
  - I have written the bulk of the extractor code from scratch
- [X] I am not the original author of this code but it is in public domain or released under [Unlicense](http://unlicense.org/) (provide reliable evidence)
  - Some of the code is inspired by @ruru4143's PR, who released it under the Unlicense according to that post. 

### What is the purpose of your *pull request*?
- [ ] Bug fix
- [ ] Improvement
- [X] New extractor
- [ ] New feature

---

### Description of your *pull request* and other information

Add support for extracting videos from [tube.tugraz.at](https://tube.tugraz.at), the lecture video site from Graz University of Technology.

Features:

- extract videos
- extract playlists
- extract restricted videos and playlists if login details are provided
- attempt fallback formats if metadata cannot be downloaded
- formats are assigned human-readable `format_id`s
- "presentation" formats are preferred over "presenter" formats

Reading the original PR ([Youtube-DL#29160](https://github.com/ytdl-org/youtube-dl/pull/29160)) might be useful, since the comments to that PR contain some discussion about the detailed design.

The original PR got stalled because of coding style issues, although the maintainer of Youtube-DL refused to elaborate on what exactly was the issue. I tried to follow the coding style guidelines as closely as possible, and I can't really see an issue.

I skimmed the YT-DLP coding style guidelines as well, and it appears nothing has changed compared to Youtube-DL's. If I misread that, and the coding style did change, please mention it so I can fix it.

### What is the purpose of your *pull request*?
- [ ] Bug fix
- [X] Improvement
- [ ] New extractor
- [ ] New feature

---

### Description of your *pull request* and other information

Adds support for [brotli](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Brotli) content encoding alongside the existing gzip and deflate (`Content-Encoding: gzip, deflate, br`). This requires either the brotli or brotlicffi module to enable.

Used by most browsers today, and sites such as YouTube seem to prefer to use it if available.

I'm not sure if this would make much difference to yt-dlp, but it seems simple to add. Open for discussion.

I haven't added it to dependencies in setup.py or requirements.txt since it may break some existing automated builds.
brotli is preferred if you are using CPython, brotlicffi elsewhere <sup>[ref](https://pypi.org/project/brotlicffi/)</sup>

entries if we want to add them:
[requirements.txt](https://github.com/coletdjnz/yt-dlp-dev/blob/yt-dlp-go-brrr-prototype/requirements.txt#L5-L6), [setup.py](https://github.com/coletdjnz/yt-dlp-dev/blob/yt-dlp-go-brrr-prototype/setup.py#L26-L29)

### What is the purpose of your *pull request*?
- [X] Bug fix
- [ ] Improvement
- [ ] New extractor
- [ ] New feature

---

### Description of your *pull request* and other information

There was a somewhat recent issue with Linux where everything using cookies from browser would fail due to an AttributeError when trying to find the keyring. It was addressed in release 2022.01.21 (for the life of me I can't find the original issue). 

The new release fixes that issue, but sort of just moved it up the chain. When attempting to use cookies from browser with the latest release I get this: 

```
 File "/usr/lib/python3.10/site-packages/yt_dlp/cookies.py", line 333, in __init__
    password = _get_linux_keyring_password(browser_keyring_name, keyring, logger)

  File "/usr/lib/python3.10/site-packages/yt_dlp/cookies.py", line 793, in _get_linux_keyring_password
    keyring = _LinuxKeyring[keyring] if keyring else _choose_linux_keyring(logger)

  File "/usr/lib/python3.10/site-packages/yt_dlp/cookies.py", line 682, in _choose_linux_keyring
    logger.debug('detected desktop environment: {}'.format(desktop_environment.name))
AttributeError: 'NoneType' object has no attribute 'name'
```

I just grabbed the last little bit of the log error. This PR addresses this issue by returning `_LinuxDesktopEnvironment.OTHER` if the `DESKTOP_SESSION` variable isn't KDE, XFCE, or Gnome.

- [X] At least skimmed through [contributing guidelines](https://github.com/yt-dlp/yt-dlp/blob/master/CONTRIBUTING.md#developer-instructions) including [yt-dlp coding conventions](https://github.com/yt-dlp/yt-dlp/blob/master/CONTRIBUTING.md#yt-dlp-coding-conventions)
- [X] [Searched](https://github.com/yt-dlp/yt-dlp/search?q=is%3Apr&type=Issues) the bugtracker for similar pull requests
- [X] Checked the code with [flake8](https://pypi.python.org/pypi/flake8)

### In order to be accepted and merged into yt-dlp each piece of code must be in public domain or released under [Unlicense](http://unlicense.org/). Check one of the following options:
- [X] I am the original author of this code and I am willing to release it under [Unlicense](http://unlicense.org/)

### What is the purpose of your *pull request*?
- [ ] Bug fix
- [X] Improvement
- [ ] New extractor
- [X] New feature

---

### Description

Add real-time progress tracking to ffmpeg operations, both for downloading (`FFmpegFD`) and post-processing (`FFmpegPostProcessor`).

#### How it works

A new `FFmpegProgressTracker` class manages ffmpeg subprocess I/O using a **Queue + Thread** pattern:

1. ffmpeg is launched with `-progress pipe:1`, which outputs machine-readable progress data to stdout
2. Two daemon threads read stdout/stderr line-by-line into thread-safe queues
3. The main thread polls at 50ms intervals, draining the queues and parsing progress blocks
4. Parsed progress (ETA, speed, bytes outputted) is reported through the existing progress hook chain

#### Changes

**`yt_dlp/postprocessor/ffmpeg.py`**
- New `FFmpegProgressTracker` class (~250 lines) handling subprocess lifecycle, progress parsing, ETA/bitrate/filesize computation
- `real_run_ffmpeg` now creates a tracker and passes `-progress pipe:1` when `information` is provided
- Progress hooks translate ffmpeg-internal status (`ffmpeg_running` → `processing`, `outputted` → `processed_bytes`)

**`yt_dlp/downloader/external.py`**
- `FFmpegFD` now uses `FFmpegProgressTracker` instead of raw `Popen`
- Always appends `-progress pipe:1` since `info_dict` is always available in the downloader context
- Progress hooks translate status (`ffmpeg_running` → `downloading`, `outputted` → `downloaded_bytes`)

**`yt_dlp/postprocessor/common.py`**
- `report_progress` handles the `processing` status with percent, speed, ETA display
- Gracefully handles minimal status dicts from `PostProcessorMetaClass.run_wrapper`

**`test/test_ffmpeg_progress.py`** — 82 unit tests covering:
- Progress regex pattern matching (single/multi stream, audio-only, N/A values)
- Time string parsing (HMS, ms/us units, fractional seconds)
- Duration computation with seek args (`-ss`, `-to`, `-t`, `-sseof`, `=` form)
- Bitrate parsing, ETA computation, filesize estimation
- Queue draining, stream accumulation, progress reporting

Closes https://github.com/yt-dlp/yt-dlp/issues/1720, https://github.com/yt-dlp/yt-dlp/issues/2826, Closes #1317
Replaces https://github.com/yt-dlp/yt-dlp/pull/1133, https://github.com/yt-dlp/yt-dlp/pull/1947

body:

### In order to be accepted and merged into yt-dlp each piece of code must be in public domain or released under [Unlicense](http://unlicense.org/). Check one of the following options:
- [x] I am the original author of this code and I am willing to release it under [Unlicense](http://unlicense.org/)
- [ ] I am not the original author of this code but it is in public domain or released under [Unlicense](http://unlicense.org/) (provide reliable evidence)

### What is the purpose of your *pull request*?
- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Improvement
- [ ] New extractor
- [ ] New feature

---

### Description of your *pull request* and other information

The existing crunchyroll beta support simply parses the page enough to find the non-beta url, then hands off to the non-beta extractor. This no longer functions for premium content for US users, since the non-beta site forcibly redirects to the beta site. See #1911.

This implements direct support for crunchyroll beta through the API, but only when a login cookie is present. Otherwise, it hands off to the non-beta extractor as usual (this is in line with what happens with an actual browser as well).

Notes:
- This is literally the first python code I have ever written. It's probably pretty horrible. I've tested that it works, but the way encoding issues are handled in python isn't familiar to me... I pretty much just tried random things until it started behaving as I wanted in that regard, so there might be problems hidden away there.
- Probably could do with some better code formatting.
- I don't know that my format generation behaves well in sufficiently many circumstances, though it behaves similarly to the non-beta crunchyroll extractor in my testing. It's mostly monkey-see-monkey-do from other extractors, rather than well-thought-out code.
- It's rather unfortunate that there's nowhere to put crunchyroll's `sequence_number` metadata in yt-dlp. This would be awfully handy to have for reasonable filename generation, as some series on the site have non-integral episode numbers. See https://beta.crunchyroll.com/series/GEXH3W8G1/the-faraway-paladin for an example.
- No series extractor at the moment, but the authorization structure is no different, so it's just a matter of interpreting the api's json output for `api_domain + '/cms/v2' + bucket + '/series/' + series_id` with the same query string.
- There are quite a few different stream types I'm not looking at here. It's possible that a different group than `adaptive_hls` is the way to go.

### Description of your *pull request* and other information

Closes #1424 

Hardcodes some data that could be retrieved from self._LOGIN_URL if cloudflare wasn't so trigger-happy.

The data does appear to be constant, but I've left the retrieval method in comments so that if they do change, breakages can be easily fixed by anyone who can use a web browser's "view source" feature and knows a little python.

If anyone knows a way to reliably get around this and download the page, that would of course be preferable.

I have not tested this with the non-beta site, as I'm in the US and thus forced to the beta site, but it *should* work, and since it wasn't working before, it is, at worst, a no-op for those users.

---

### Description of your *pull request* and other information

This should deal with issue #2519 , including the improvements I proposed. I have tested the changes in a couple of channels containing the problematic video IDs, plus a few livestreams, and everything seems to work as intended. Regarding the video IDs getting truncated in the download log, I must have misremembered it because I could not reproduce anything like that. The download archive seems to work normally.

* Fixed _VALID_URL regex (now it looks explicitly for an optional third digit block in the video ID)
* Added '__plataform=web' to cloudac queries. Now referers and access token cookies are probably no longer needed, but I do not dare remove the code yet. _Just in case_.
* Added extra metadata: timestamp, duration and thumbnails for VODs, and timestamp (technically stream start time) for live streams.
* Added graceful(-ish) failure if a video or channel is not found or if access is restricted, using raise_no_formats. It now returns a brief error message and the error code found in the json, and hides the exception details unless -v is used.

This PR fixes the following bugs:

It also updates the test cases.

Unauthenticated requests from yt-dlp get the `sharedData` structure and the bad duration; authenticated requests from yt-dlp, and unauthenticated requests from everything else I have tried, get [the `additionalData` structure](https://github.com/ytdl-org/youtube-dl/pull/22880) and no duration. I have given up debugging this due to Instagram's absurdly aggressive rate-limiting.)

Thank you!

body:
The regex was trying to be too smart. Especially the `<x(a|b|.+)+>` pattern to detect attributes is terrible since it matches every `x` tag. The inability to use `[^>]` for `script` makes it worse and results in huge intermediate matches. Breaking up the regex makes memory use negligible while also giving 50-300x perf on random pages (from my naive profiling)

I have tested `iframe` and `glomex-player`. But can't find any example of `script`/`div`

@zmousm

Fixes #2512

title: catch BlockingIOError and report why ID was skipped
body:
### Before submitting a *pull request* make sure you have:
- [x] At least skimmed through [contributing guidelines](https://github.com/yt-dlp/yt-dlp/blob/master/CONTRIBUTING.md#developer-instructions) including [yt-dlp coding conventions](https://github.com/yt-dlp/yt-dlp/blob/master/CONTRIBUTING.md#yt-dlp-coding-conventions)
- [x] [Searched](https://github.com/yt-dlp/yt-dlp/search?q=is%3Apr&type=Issues) the bugtracker for similar pull requests
- [x] Checked the code with [flake8](https://pypi.python.org/pypi/flake8)

### In order to be accepted and merged into yt-dlp each piece of code must be in public domain or released under [Unlicense](http://unlicense.org/). Check one of the following options:
- [x] I am the original author of this code and I am willing to release it under [Unlicense](http://unlicense.org/)
- [ ] I am not the original author of this code but it is in public domain or released under [Unlicense](http://unlicense.org/) (provide reliable evidence)

### What is the purpose of your *pull request*?
- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Improvement
- [ ] New extractor
- [ ] New feature

---

With the addition of advisory locking in #1066 this change is necessary to gracefully download the next video when a yt-dlp process hits an advisory lock `BlockingIOError`. Without this patch, when two yt-dlp processes are launched with either the same playlist or different playlists that happen to have at least one same ID, if one of the yt-dlp processes encounters a `BlockingIOError` the process exits. With this patch, the process that would have exited instead continues on to the next video, and the first process (this is also the case without this patch) finishes it's current ID download and produces the expected merged output file.

Videos with fragments also work as expected.

Implementation note: It is important that the yt-dlp process that encountered the `BlockingIOError` move on to the next video, rather than attempt to download the "next unlocked" part of the ID (such as the audio track) because that situation results in a corner case where the first yt-dlp process also hits a `BlockingIOError` when it finishes the part it was downloading and attempts to download the (perhaps still advisory locked) audio track. This corner case results in both processes not producing a merged output file. This corner case does not exist with or without this patch.

### Description of your *pull request* and other information

resolving #2523

I also added another subtitle track that notes when a screen share began or ended

a few issues I encountered in this extractor

the automatic password entering is no longer working, this is due to a new "defense" mechanism set by zoom
it include extra headers in the http request for the video page that are generated by a js file that is also generated based on a seed
example of the js code:
https://hdq-colman-ac.zoom.us/assets/zm_bundle.js?seed=AADx7N1-AQAAsr6kOeqMVkDZFrY_h7kUEdue172Pbg3WR8ywwtLBJedku_Fm&uQHR71Sqnk--z=q

I tried to crack it and got pretty far, but I want to close this feature first
we can bypass this issue by extracting cookies from the browser after entering the passcode ourselves, [I use an extention](https://github.com/rotemdan/ExportCookies) because the `--cookies-from-browser` didn't work for me for some reason

another issue is that the zoom recording provided as test is no longer usable, it now requires a captcha and form filling to enter
so I removed it, I do not have a public meeting to replace it with
it maybe be usable if the test uses browser cookies after one registers to the zoom call from their browser, but I don't know how the test framework operates

also, I noticed that if I do not state output format and download all formats, they have the same name and so only one of them is downloaded, is that the intended behavior?

### Description of your *pull request* and other information

AfreecaTV livestreams may be protected by a password. This PR updates the extractor to support the existing `--video-password` param, and to raise an ExtractorError if no password is provided and the livestream metadata indicates that a password is required.

Due to the temporary nature of livestreams, I don't know if there's a way to meaningfully unit test this; I can at least confirm by manual testing that it:
- [x] successfully downloads when the correct password is provided,
- [x] provides a suitable error if a password is required and not provided, and
- [x] successfully downloads unprotected videos as before.

---

### Description of your *pull request* and other information

This adds an extractor for the videos hosted on the <womens.afl> website, for an Australian sports league. Videos appear to be downloadable from anywhere (they work from up here in Canada!)

Individual videos are downloadable already, they are standard Brightcove embeds, however the site provides several index pages with playlists, including recent match replays, highlights, and other sections, though unfortunately with minimal consistency in how they're laid out. Some are under a `videos` prefix, and others are right on the root.

For that reason, I've made the regex explicit about what pages it supports, though I am open to suggestions for a better approach.

Secondly, the tests are using playlists which are actively updated - there aren't really any which aren't updated frequently - is there a better way to write these such that they don't degrade over time?

- [ ] I am not the original author of this code but it is in public domain or released under [Unlicense](http://unlicense.org/) (provide reliable evidence)

### What is the purpose of your *pull request*?
- [ ] Bug fix
- [x] Improvement
- [ ] New extractor
- [ ] New feature

---

### Description of your *pull request* and other information

When downloading a HLS video I noted that many fragments were missing. Looking at the log, these fragments were given transient 429s in order to suggest to the client that it should back off and try again after a few seconds. However, the current retries happen as fast as we can go, which means those fragments just end up skipped.

This patch adds support for exponential backoff when doing fragment retries, which increases the chance that the fragment will eventually be provided in such cases. This is disabled by default, and can be enabled with --fragment-retry-backoff.

Example delays in seconds are as follows with the default 10 retries:

    >>> for i in range(10): print("{:.2f}".format(0.25 * 1.5 ** i))
    0.25
    0.38
    0.56
    0.84
    1.27
    1.90
    2.85
    4.27
    6.41
    9.61
